text: The surgeon navigates through the abdominal cavity, encountering and carefully dissecting omental adhesions at the midline. The small bowel is visible with multiple adhesions, some of which are freed to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon is taking down small bowel and omental adhesions to the colostomy. The rectal stump is identified and mobilized with ease. The surgeon works laterally to mobilize the descending colon up to the splenic flexure, preparing to take down the colostomy for further mobilization.
The surgeon approaches the fascia and begins to clear the ostomy from it, having completed all possible mobilization up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon describes the port placement for the robotic colostomy reversal, with a 12mm stapler port in the right lower quadrant, a camera port approximately 5-6 centimeters to the right of the umbilicus, and two 8mm ports extending upwards. An assistive port is positioned in the right flank area.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, encountering significant scarring in the area. Using the most cephalad eight millimeter port, the surgeon clears the mesentery from the colon, preparing to staple across the colon.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to prevent the anvil from falling too far into the colon. An assistant at the bedside uses a Kelly clamp to slide the anvil into the colostomy. The surgeon ensures the anvil is positioned low enough to avoid stapling across it, then uses a robotic stapler with a blue load to transect the colon at the level of the fascia.

The surgeon performs a transection at the staple line in the middle of the descending colon, creating a colotomy. The anvil is then pushed through without using the plastic spike, ensuring precise control over the suture's exit point.

The surgeon enters through the gastrocolic ligament into the lesser sac to mobilize the splenic flexure. The omentum is visibly scarred to the spleen on the right side, making lateral access challenging. The approach is from supracolic, medial to lateral.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure and proceeds with medialization of the colon to ensure adequate length. The rectal stump is exposed, showing some remaining sigmoid colon, which is resected due to its mobility. The surgeon trims the top of the rectal stump to facilitate stapler passage, noting its scarring and lack of compliance. An assistant is present at the bedside, but the surgeon handles the stapler. The surgeon sews the additional specimen to the colostomy for removal later. The posterior rectus sheath is visible, and the surgeon performs a robotic posterior closure, planning to close the anterior fascia after colostomy removal.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a 29 EEA stapler, passing rectal sizers from 25 to 33 to facilitate the process. A rigid proctoscope is used to conduct a leak test above the level of the anastomosis, confirming the integrity of the connection. The robot is then undocked, and the colostomy is excised, followed by closure of the anterior fascia.
